The second one is in any list there are always hyper-responsives, people that will

often sell to a much higher percentage because of the power of the small lists.
Types of lists.
This is important stuff. There are prospect lists and buyer lists. Buyer lists are just
people who have bought from you. Prospect lists are people who have not bought
from you yet.
In my experience, someone on your buyer list is worth 15 times what someone on
your prospect list is worth. They will respond 15 times more; the response rate
will be 15 times higher on average. This will vary; it could be lower or it could be
higher, but it is a dramatic difference.
You generally don’t segment by lead source, but sometimes you do. A list that’s
driven from people who saw you do some public speaking and they gave you a
business card or somehow opted into your list after you spoke, versus whether
you bought some traffic on Google and sent that traffic to a squeeze page, they’re
very different lists.
If someone saw you speak that’s generally one of the warmest lists. If someone
came to you through an endorsed mailing through a joint venture partner where
a joint venture partner said, “Go check out Jeff’s stuff, Jeff is awesome,” that’s a
warmer list than someone who came in through advertising. Social media tends
to be a little warmer than advertising. In general, advertising is one of the colder
lists, but it still can be good. You always want to be mindful of your lead source.
www.getwsodownload.com

I use double opt-in. For most markets, I think it makes sense. Certainly in my
market it makes sense because so many people have put in garbage names in the
teaching people about business online and the internet marketing world. So the
double opt-in keeps your list cleaner, it stops people maliciously adding in bogus

You will lose people, you will have people that don’t confirm. Generally I lose

email addresses anyway, so there’s no way they would ever confirm. Also, if
they’re not even going to click the confirmation, they’re probably not going to be
a good subscriber in any case.

squeeze page. But you don’t want to just send them into a launch sequence about
learning to play guitar if they joined wanting to know about dissertations.
“How to provide real value for my customers and potential customers? How
often can I send emails without being annoying?”
How do you provide real value? By, first of all, treating them like real human,
treating them like friends. How would you want to be treated? If you joined
someone’s list about whatever your topic is you want to hear about things that
relate to you, your problems, your hopes, your dreams, your fears, your
aspirations, your desires. As long as you’re providing stuff that speaks to those
things, the results people want, and as long as you’re providing value around
those things, then you’re providing value.
It’s always a great question to ponder, “What is value and how can I create
value?” so I love this question.
How often to send emails without being annoying… most of the time on the
spectrum of too many emails or not enough emails, most people send not enough
emails. These days people get so many emails that it’s just usually not a problem.
I think for almost any market, weekly is certainly not too often. I don’t think twice
weekly is too often. There are some markets where daily maybe is too often. But I
think somewhere between daily and weekly is the sweet spot for most markets
and most lists.
“What is most important about the choice in email list marketing hosting
services?”
It’s deliverability. Deliverability is absolutely number one. Speed of delivery is
important. And then the functionality within the service.

I already talked about why I love AWeber. I really like the looks of Office

“I have a fairly small but trusted list. How do I best keep them from dropping off
as I know choose to go forward and grow?”
If you’re going to go forward and grow and go in a slightly different direction,
you’re always going to have some people drop off. That’s okay. It’s all about self
selection. If someone is not interested in where you’re going, then they’re not
interested so working them to keep them on your list isn’t any good. You want
the people that are interested in you and your voice, your personality, and your
content. So if you lose some of them, that’s okay, you’ll replace them with new
people.
“What are your top one to three favorite set and forget list building strategies?
Strategies that don’t require work, but aren’t dependent on the latest trick and
will last a good few years.”
Having a great squeeze page with a great offer. I’ve always been a fan of
publishing, that’s always been my go-to thing. I try to publish in the market –
blogs, videos, social media – to create value out in the market and then have that
content go back to my squeeze pages.
I also have some low level amount of advertising going on, like with Google
Adwords or with Facebook. I’m a big fan of that. Those are the two main things.
“What’s your number one strategy for training the list to interact with you?”
Asking for interaction is really important. You train your list to interact by getting
them to interact. I like to lower the bar, so I don’t send people to survey, I usually
just ask people to respond to my emails with that little P.S. trick that I gave you
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 52 of 54 --

52
©Internet Alchemy Inc. 2013
All rights Reserved
earlier, “P.S. I really want to know what you think about this. Hit the reply button
now and let me know.” That’s a really great one.
Getting people just to click is a way of getting them to interact. Periodically
sending them cool content, even content that’s not on your site. If you send them
to a YouTube video, a lot of times they might be thinking “this is just another
offer,” but if they see a YouTube URL they know it’s not an offer, they know it’s a
video.
So find a really cool video and say, “Check out this really cool video I found about
_,” and give them the link. When you give them the link, don’t have a fancy
click here for whatever, have it be a YouTube link so they actually see that it’s a
YouTube link and know that they’re clicking on a YouTube link. Then you’re
training them to click, but it’s a non-threatening click. That’s a great trick – and it’s
not a really a “trick,” you’re just adding value.
I do send people to surveys. One of the things that keeps people interacting is
that revelation of self, being a human and letting them know you’re very real,
letting them know you have problems, letting them know you have hopes and
dreams, letting them know you have kids that you’re worried about, or if you
don’t have kids that you have friends or whatever.
Tell them about what you’re doing on vacation. Don’t give them a long 800 word
“this is what I did on my summer vacation,” but just tell them a little bit about
yourself. Bounce them over to your blog, ask them for a comment on your blog,
ask them for a comment on Facebook, bounce them between Facebook and your
blog.
Mix up the ways that you ask for interaction; ask for the click, send them to a
survey, send them to a Facebook post, ask them to reply to the email. The best
way to get an interaction is to give them multiple ways to interact and just ask for
that interaction and do it early. When they join your list, do it fairly early in one of
your first few emails just to get that start of interaction going.
So there’s your homework.
